content:
# Luke Chapter 23 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 117-119 | **Source Images:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_023_page_001.jpg, LUK_023_page_002.jpg, LUK_023_page_003.jpg

---

### Jesus Before Pilate (vv. 1-5)

**1** Then the whole assembly rose and brought him before Pilate. **2** They began making their case against him: "We found this man undermining our nation — telling people not to pay taxes to Caesar, and claiming to be the Messiah, a king."

**3** Pilate asked him, "Are you the king of the Jews?" Jesus answered, "Those are your words."

**4** Pilate told the chief priests and the crowds, "I find no basis for a charge against this man." **5** But they pressed harder: "He stirs up the people with his teaching all across Judea — he started in Galilee and now he's come all the way here!"

### Jesus Before Herod (vv. 6-12)

**6** When Pilate heard this, he asked whether the man was a Galilean. **7** Learning that Jesus fell under Herod's jurisdiction, he sent him over to Herod, who happened to be in Jerusalem at that time.

**8** Herod was thrilled to see Jesus. He had been wanting to meet him for a long time, having heard so much about him, and he was hoping to see him perform some sign. **9** He peppered Jesus with question after question, but Jesus gave him no answer at all. **10** The chief priests and the scribes stood there, accusing him vehemently. **11** Then Herod and his soldiers treated Jesus with contempt and made a spectacle of him. They dressed him in a magnificent robe and sent him back to Pilate. **12** That very day, Herod and Pilate became friends — before this, there had been hostility between them.

### Pilate Declares Jesus Innocent (vv. 13-25)

**13** Pilate called together the chief priests, the leaders, and the people. **14** He said to them, "You brought this man to me as someone who incites the people to rebellion. I have examined him in your presence and found no grounds for the charges you are bringing against him. **15** Neither has Herod — he sent him back to us. Clearly, he has done nothing deserving death. **16** So I will have him flogged and then release him." **17** [He was obligated to release one prisoner to them during the festival.]

**18** But the whole crowd roared in unison, "Away with this man! Release Barabbas to us!" **19** (Barabbas had been thrown into prison for a riot that broke out in the city, and for murder.)

**20** Pilate, wanting to release Jesus, appealed to them again. **21** But they kept shouting, "Crucify! Crucify him!"

**22** A third time he said to them, "Why? What crime has this man committed? I have found nothing in him that warrants a death sentence. I will have him flogged and release him." **23** But they kept up the pressure, demanding with loud shouts that he be crucified — and their voices won out.

**24** Pilate handed down the verdict they wanted. **25** He released the man who had been jailed for riot and murder — the one they were asking for — and surrendered Jesus to their will.

### The Road to the Cross (vv. 26-31)

**26** As they led him away, they seized a man from Cyrene named Simon, who was coming in from the countryside, and forced him to carry the cross behind Jesus.

**27** A huge crowd of people followed him, including women who were beating their chests and wailing for him. **28** Jesus turned to them and said, "Daughters of Jerusalem, don't weep for me. Weep for yourselves and for your children. **29** The days are coming when people will say, 'Blessed are the women who never bore children — the wombs that never carried and the breasts that never nursed!' **30** Then they will say to the mountains, 'Fall on us!' and to the hills, 'Cover us!' **31** For if they do these things when the wood is green, what will happen when it is dry?"

### The Crucifixion (vv. 32-38)

**32** Two others — both criminals — were also led out to be executed with him.

**33** When they arrived at the place called The Skull, they crucified him there, along with the criminals — one on his right, one on his left.

**34** ⸂Jesus said, "Father, forgive them — they don't know what they are doing."⸃ They divided up his clothing by casting lots.

**35** The people stood watching. The leaders sneered at him: "He saved others — let him save himself, if he really is God's Messiah, the Chosen One." **36** The soldiers also mocked him, coming up to offer him sour wine, **37** saying, "If you're the king of the Jews, save yourself!" **38** There was also an inscription over him: THIS IS THE KING OF THE JEWS.

### The Two Criminals (vv. 39-43)

**39** One of the criminals hanging there hurled insults at him: "Aren't you the Messiah? Save yourself — and us while you're at it!"

**40** But the other one rebuked him: "Don't you even fear God? You're under the same sentence he is! **41** We deserve what we're getting — we earned this. But this man has done nothing wrong." **42** Then he said, "Jesus, remember me when you come into your kingdom."

**43** Jesus said to him, "I tell you the truth — today you will be with me in Paradise."

### The Death of Jesus (vv. 44-49)

**44** By now it was about noon, and darkness fell over the whole land until three in the afternoon — **45** the sun's light failed. The curtain of the temple was torn right down the middle.

**46** Then Jesus cried out in a loud voice, "Father, into your hands I place my spirit!" And with those words, he breathed his last.

**47** When the centurion saw what had happened, he praised God and said, "There is no doubt — this was a righteous man." **48** And all the crowds who had gathered to watch this spectacle, when they saw what had taken place, went home beating their chests in grief.

**49** But all who knew him stood at a distance watching these things — including the women who had followed him from Galilee.

### The Burial of Jesus (vv. 50-56)

**50** Now there was a man named Joseph, a member of the council, a good and upright man. **51** He had not agreed with the council's decision or their actions. He came from the Judean town of Arimathea, and he was waiting expectantly for the kingdom of God.

**52** This man went to Pilate and asked for the body of Jesus. **53** He took it down, wrapped it in linen cloth, and laid it in a rock-cut tomb where no one had ever been buried. **54** It was Preparation Day, and the Sabbath was about to begin.

**55** The women who had come with Jesus from Galilee followed along and saw the tomb and how his body was placed in it. **56** Then they went home and prepared spices and perfumed oils. And on the Sabbath they rested, as the commandment required.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### Overall Approach
Luke 23 is the narrative climax of the Gospel — trial, crucifixion, death, burial. I aimed to keep the pace relentless where Luke keeps it relentless (the crowd scenes, the shouting) and to slow down where he slows down (the words from the cross, the moment of death). Every sentence should hit the reader with the weight of what is happening.

### Verse-by-Verse Notes on Significant Dynamic Choices

**v. 2 — "undermining our nation"**: The Greek diastrephonta to ethnos is literally "perverting/turning the nation." I rendered this as "undermining our nation" because "perverting" in modern English has shifted meaning, and "undermining" captures the political accusation the leaders are making before Pilate — that Jesus is destabilizing the social order.

**v. 5 — "pressed harder"**: Greek epischyon, "they were insistent/grew stronger." The dynamic force is escalation. "Pressed harder" conveys the mounting pressure on Pilate.

**v. 8 — "thrilled"**: Greek echare lian, "rejoiced greatly." Herod's excitement is not reverent — it is the curiosity of a spectator who wants entertainment. "Thrilled" captures the shallow quality of his eagerness.

**v. 9 — "peppered Jesus with question after question"**: The Greek eperotaen en logois hikanois is literally "questioned him in many/considerable words." The dynamic sense is persistent, voluminous questioning met with total silence.

**v. 11 — "made a spectacle of him"**: Greek exouthenesas, "having treated with contempt/made nothing of." The word carries the force of public humiliation — reducing someone to nothing for the crowd's amusement.

**v. 12 — "became friends"**: This verse preserves one of Luke's bitter ironies — two political rivals bonding over the abuse of an innocent man. I kept the matter-of-fact tone, letting the irony speak for itself.

**v. 17 — Bracketed verse**: This verse (about releasing a prisoner at the festival) is a textual variant. Sinaiticus includes it, though its placement varies across manuscripts and some omit it entirely. I have retained it in brackets to reflect its uncertain status.

**v. 23 — "their voices won out"**: Greek katischyon hai phonai auton, "their voices prevailed." This is a striking phrase — Luke frames the injustice as a contest of volume. I used "won out" to capture the sense that sheer noise overwhelmed justice.

**v. 24 — "handed down the verdict they wanted"**: Greek epekrinen genesthai to aitema auton, literally "he decreed that their demand should happen." The dynamic force is that Pilate gave them their sentence — not his own judgment, but theirs. The phrasing emphasizes Pilate's capitulation.

**v. 31 — "when the wood is green...when it is dry"**: This is a proverbial saying. The green wood is the living, the dry wood is the dead — or alternatively, the present (when there is still some restraint) versus the future (when there will be none). I retained the imagery because it is vivid and arresting in English as well, and the meaning is best conveyed by letting the metaphor stand.

### The Major Textual Variant: Verse 34

**v. 34 — "Father, forgive them — they don't know what they are doing"**: This is one of the most significant textual variants in the New Testament. In Codex Sinaiticus, the original scribe (scribe A) **omitted** this saying. It was later added by a corrector. The saying is also absent from P75, Vaticanus (original hand), and several other early witnesses, while present in the second corrector of Sinaiticus, Alexandrinus, Bezae, and most later manuscripts.

I have included the verse because (a) a Sinaiticus corrector added it, meaning it was known and considered authentic early on, and (b) it has massive weight in the received tradition. However, I marked it with half-brackets (⸂ ⸃) to signal its disputed status. The omission in the earliest stratum of witnesses is striking and may reflect early theological discomfort (did Jesus pray for those who crucified him?) or scribal accident. Readers should be aware that this beloved saying may not have been in Luke's original text.

**v. 42 — "when you come into your kingdom"**: Sinaiticus reads eis ten basileian sou ("into your kingdom") rather than the variant en te basileia sou ("in your kingdom"). The difference matters: "into" suggests arrival — Jesus entering his reign — while "in" suggests an already-existing realm. The Sinaiticus reading has a more future-oriented, eschatological force.

**v. 43 — "Paradise"**: Greek paradeisos. I retained this word untranslated because it has entered English and carries its own theological weight. The word originally referred to an enclosed garden or royal park, and in Jewish thought had come to mean the blessed resting place of the righteous dead.

**v. 45 — "the sun's light failed"**: Greek tou heliou eklipontos. Sinaiticus uses the participle of ekleipo, meaning "to fail" or "to give out" — the sun's light ceased. Some manuscripts read eskotisthe, "was darkened." The Sinaiticus reading has a more absolute quality: the light did not merely dim; it gave out entirely.

**v. 46 — "Father, into your hands I place my spirit"**: Greek paratithemai to pneuma mou. The verb is a present middle — "I am entrusting/depositing." It is an act of deliberate, conscious surrender. "I place" captures the voluntary nature of this final breath — Jesus is not having his life taken; he is handing it over.

**v. 47 — "righteous man"**: Greek dikaios. Where Matthew and Mark have the centurion say "Son of God," Luke has "righteous" (dikaios). This is theologically significant — Luke's centurion recognizes Jesus' innocence and moral character, which fits Luke's sustained emphasis throughout the trial narrative that Jesus has done nothing wrong.

**v. 48 — "beating their chests in grief"**: Greek typtomenoi ta stethe, literally "striking their chests." In the ancient Mediterranean world, this was a visible sign of mourning and remorse. "Beating their chests in grief" makes the cultural gesture legible to modern readers.

---

*Translation produced by PNEUMA from Codex Sinaiticus (4th century CE), the earliest complete manuscript of the New Testament. Read directly from manuscript facsimile images, PDF pages 117-119.*
